title: Selectors, Cardinality, Unknown Values, and Typed Extraction · Mastery Check
summary: A release reviewer compares near-miss TypeScript decisions for selectors, cardinality, unknown values, and typed extraction and accepts only compiler plus runtime changed-case evidence, explicit non-claims, and named network, DNS, browser, accessibility, security, load, legal-review, and production transfer gates. Planned artifact: Record independent decisions and explanations for runtime-validated extraction contract. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice…
objectives: Define selector scope, expected cardinality, fallback order, exclusions, and source location before extracting.
Represent absent, empty, malformed, duplicate, conflicting, and admitted values as distinct outcomes.
Keep attributes, text, JSON-LD, configuration, and plugin output unknown until validators produce domain values.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

This does not satisfy Drift Detection for runtime-validated extraction contract.
Run the Drift Detection boundary case for runtime-validated extraction contract, inspect the actual mechanism, then accept or repair the claim.

steps.title: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Drift Detection
steps.instruction:
Write the quiz evidence note for Drift Detection: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A quiz TypeScript team handles case 7023 during an isolated browser, package, and restore smoke. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove selector cardinality, missingness, unknown runtime validation, field normalization, and drift alarms; URL, robots, body, parser, frontier, privacy, accessibility, and report budgets stay explicit; then change one HTML repair, Cheerio mode, selector, unknown value, record schema, link relation, or metadata case and retain rejected evidence. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Use fixtures, cardinality histograms, rejection rates, schema versions, and changed markup to surface selector drift.

Evidence target: Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Drift Detection.
steps.why: Retrieving Drift Detection through the concrete runtime-validated extraction contract case exposes causal gaps that recognition alone can hide. For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend one difficult decision · Quiz Drift Detection, keep Drift Detection connected to executable TypeScript authorization, URL, runtime admission, Fetch, Promise, browser, accessibility, report, package, and recovery decision evidence.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Use fixtures, cardinality histograms, rejection rates, schema versions, and changed markup to surface selector drift.
